title: Creating Labels from the Data Itself
concept: Self-supervised learning often works by manufacturing targets from the input itself, such as masked words, missing image patches, or next-step prediction in a sequence.

exercises.prompt: Recap: In self-supervised learning, where does the target usually come from?

options.text: From structure already present in the data.
From a random number generator with no connection to the input.
Only from a human writing labels one by one.

exercises.explain: Right. The input itself is used to create a prediction target.

cards.title: Turn one example into its own quiz
cards.body: A clever trick in self-supervised learning is that one raw example can play two roles at once: part of it becomes the clue, and another part becomes the answer. One image, sentence, or sequence can therefore generate its own practice problem.

cards.title: How labels get created automatically
cards.body: Instead of collecting labels from people, a model can create auto-generated targets by hiding, shifting, cropping, or reordering parts of the data. For example, the original hidden word, missing patch, or next event becomes the thing the model must predict.

cards.title: Fold part of the worksheet over
cards.body: Imagine taking a worksheet and folding one section so you cannot see it. You then try to reconstruct what is hidden. The worksheet already contained the answer — you just turned it into a challenge by blocking part of it from view.

cards.title: Missing patches and next steps
cards.body: In vision, a model might see most of an image while one patch is hidden, then predict what belongs in the blank area. In sequence data, it might read earlier steps and predict the next one. In both cases, the training target came directly from the original example.

cards.title: The target is hidden, not invented
cards.body: A common myth is that self-generated labels are made up or unreliable. But in many self-supervised setups, the target is not random at all — it is the real missing piece from the original data, temporarily hidden so the model has to reason about it.

cards.title: Three common self-made targets
cards.body: Different data types create different prediction games.

left.text:
Sentence with blanks
Image with a hidden patch
Sequence up to time step 5

right.text:
The masked word
The missing visual content
The next token or next step

exercises.prompt: Which example shows a label being created from the data itself?

options.text: Hide the last word in a sentence and use the original word as the prediction target.
Ask a human to score every image from 1 to 10.
Delete the training set and start from scratch.
Train only on labels with no original inputs.

exercises.explain: Correct. The hidden original word becomes the target, so the supervision comes from the sentence itself.

exercises.prompt: True or false: If you cover a square patch in an image, the original hidden patch can serve as the target the model tries to predict.
exercises.explain: True. The model learns by reconstructing a real part of the original image.

exercises.sentence: When a word is masked, the original word becomes the model's prediction ___.
exercises.explain: Target is correct. The hidden original piece is what the model is trying to recover.

exercises.prompt: Which situation best fits the idea of self-generated labels?

options.text: A speech model listens to the first part of an audio clip and predicts the chunk that comes next.
A company hires reviewers to tag each clip as positive or negative.
An engineer manually rewrites every audio file into a summary before training.

exercises.explain: Exactly. The future chunk comes from the clip itself, so the data is generating the supervision.

exercises.prompt: Why can next-step prediction train a model even when nobody has written labels for the dataset?

options.text: Because the later step in the sequence already exists in the raw data and can be used as the target.
Because next-step prediction removes the need for a loss function.
Because unlabeled data can never contain useful order or structure.
Because the model ignores the input and predicts from pure chance.

exercises.explain: Exactly. The answer is already present later in the sequence, so the data itself supplies the supervision.
